abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown stunning results in complex tasks, yet they often operate in isolation, failing to learn from past experiences. Existing memory-based methods primarily store raw trajectories, which are often redundant and noise-heavy. This prevents agents from extracting high-level, reusable behavioral patterns that are essential for generalization. In this paper, we propose SkillRL, a framework that bridges the gap between raw experience and policy improvement through automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution. Our approach introduces an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library SkillBank, an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that allows the skill library to co-evolve with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning. These innovations significantly reduce the token footprint while enhancing reasoning utility. Experimental results on ALFWorld, WebShop and seven search-augmented tasks demonstrate that SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines over 15.3% and maintaining robustness as task complexity increases. Code is available at this this https URL.

keyInsights: Abstracting experiences into high-level, hierarchical skills provides superior performance and context efficiency for LLM agents compared to storing or compressing raw interaction trajectories.

A dynamic and recursive skill evolution mechanism, where the skill library co-evolves with the agent's policy, is critical for continuous improvement, adaptability, and effectively addressing emergent failure modes.
Initial Supervised Fine-Tuning on skill-augmented reasoning traces is essential for enabling the base LLM agent to effectively utilize and integrate abstract skills into its decision-making process.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) agents often operate episodically, failing to learn and generalize from past experiences, which limits their scalability and adaptability.
Existing memory-augmented LLM agents predominantly store raw, verbose, and noisy interaction trajectories, leading to "context bloat" and inefficient extraction of high-level behavioral patterns.
Current memory mechanisms typically function as external references without fundamentally adapting the agent's internal policy or evolving to address new failure modes and challenges.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art success rates, including 89.9% on ALFWorld, 72.7% on WebShop, and an average score of 47.1% on search-augmented QA tasks.
Outperformed strong baselines, including GPT-4o by 41.9% and Gemini-2.5-Pro by 29.6% on ALFWorld, demonstrating that intelligent skill learning can compensate for model scale.

Reduced prompt token usage by approximately 10.3% compared to raw memory retrieval, while also exhibiting faster convergence and a dynamic expansion of the skill library from 55 to 100 skills during training.
solution: Employs a teacher model to distill raw interaction trajectories, including both successful episodes and failures, into concise "strategic patterns" and "failure lessons."

Constructs a hierarchical SKILLBANK that organizes distilled knowledge into general skills (universal strategies) and task-specific skills, designed for efficient retrieval and significant token compression.

Utilizes Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for a cold-start initialization, followed by recursive skill evolution during Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to dynamically refine the skill library and agent policy based on ongoing performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and collaborators developed SKILLRL, a framework enabling Large Language Model (LLM) agents to learn and recursively evolve abstract skills from diverse experiences. SKILLRL achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 89.9% success rate on ALFWorld, 72.7% on WebShop, and an average score of 47.1% on search-augmented QA tasks, outperforming larger closed-source models and memory-augmented baselines.

title: SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
We introduce \emph{Memento-Skills}, a generalist, continually-learnable LLM agent system that functions as an \emph{agent-designing agent}: it autonomously constructs, adapts, and improves task-specific agents through experience. The system is built on a memory-based reinforcement learning framework with \emph{stateful prompts}, where reusable skills (stored as structured markdown files) serve as persistent, evolving memory. These skills encode both behaviour and context, enabling the agent to carry forward knowledge across interactions.
Starting from simple elementary skills (like Web search and terminal operations), the agent continually improves via the \emph{Read--Write Reflective Learning} mechanism introduced in \emph{Memento~2}~\cite{wang2025memento2}. In the \emph{read} phase, a behaviour-trainable skill router selects the most relevant skill conditioned on the current stateful prompt; in the \emph{write} phase, the agent updates and expands its skill library based on new experience. This closed-loop design enables \emph{continual learning without updating LLM parameters}, as all adaptation is realised through the evolution of externalised skills and prompts.
Unlike prior approaches that rely on human-designed agents, Memento-Skills enables a generalist agent to \emph{design agents end-to-end} for new tasks. Through iterative skill generation and refinement, the system progressively improves its own capabilities. Experiments on the \emph{General AI Assistants} benchmark and \emph{Humanity's Last Exam} demonstrate sustained gains, achieving 26.2\% and 116.2\% relative improvements in overall accuracy, respectively. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Continual learning for LLMs can be effectively achieved by evolving an external skill library without modifying the core LLM parameters, enabling agents to autonomously design and improve task-specific behaviors.

Framing skill retrieval as a one-step Markov Decision Process and optimizing for behavioral utility through offline reinforcement learning significantly enhances the effectiveness of skill selection over semantic similarity alone.
Structured, domain-aligned skill libraries facilitate substantial cross-task generalization, allowing skills refined in one context to be reused effectively for unseen tasks within the same domain.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agents are often deployed as "frozen models," preventing them from continually learning from deployment experiences due to the high computational and data costs of parameter updates.
Frozen LLMs are stateless, lacking the ability to retain knowledge across interactions or adapt efficiently to new tasks, leading to suboptimal performance over time.
Traditional memory-augmented LLMs often use passive storage of interaction logs, and semantic retrieval mechanisms frequently fail to prioritize behavioral utility for effective skill selection.
results: The system achieved a 66.0% test accuracy on the GAIA benchmark, a 13.7 percentage-point improvement over the 52.3% baseline without skill optimization, and more than doubled the baseline performance on HLE, reaching 38.7% accuracy versus 17.9%.

Over multiple learning rounds, training success rates consistently improved, with GAIA increasing from 65.1% to 91.6% and HLE from 30.8% to 54.5%, demonstrating continuous refinement of the skill library.

The behavior-aligned skill router, Memento-Qwen, outperformed semantic baselines with a Recall@1 of 0.60 (vs. 0.32 for BM25 and 0.54 for Qwen3-Embedding) and increased the end-to-end judge success rate to 0.80.
solution: A "Memento-Skills" system that externalizes learning into an evolving skill library, defining skills as persistent, structured markdown units encapsulating executable code and contextual prompts.

Implements a closed-loop Read–Write Reflective Learning mechanism, drawing from the Stateful Reflective Decision Process (SRDP), where the agent iteratively evaluates execution outcomes to update, rewrite, and discover new skills.

Develops a behavior-aligned skill router, trained with one-step offline reinforcement learning using a multi-positive InfoNCE loss, to optimize for execution success rather than purely semantic similarity during skill retrieval.
paper_summary.summary: Memento-Skills introduces a system for Large Language Model (LLM) agents to continually learn and improve without updating core model parameters, treating reusable skills as an external, evolving memory. The system achieved a 13.7 percentage-point increase in test accuracy on the GAIA benchmark and more than doubled the baseline performance on the HLE dataset, enabling autonomous agent design and adaptation.

title: Memento-Skills: Let Agents Design Agents

abstract: Agent Skills are structured packages of procedural knowledge that augment LLM agents at inference time. Despite rapid adoption, there is no standard way to measure whether they actually help. We present SkillsBench, a benchmark of 86 tasks across 11 domains paired with curated Skills and deterministic verifiers. Each task is evaluated under three conditions: no Skills, curated Skills, and self-generated Skills. We test 7 agent-model configurations over 7,308 trajectories. Curated Skills raise average pass rate by 16.2 percentage points(pp), but effects vary widely by domain (+4.5pp for Software Engineering to +51.9pp for Healthcare) and 16 of 84 tasks show negative deltas. Self-generated Skills provide no benefit on average, showing that models cannot reliably author the procedural knowledge they benefit from consuming. Focused Skills with 2--3 modules outperform comprehensive documentation, and smaller models with Skills can match larger models without them.

keyInsights: Curated Agent Skills provide substantial but variable performance benefits to LLM agents, with an average improvement of +16.2 percentage points, depending on the agent-model combination and domain.
Self-generated Skills by LLMs yield negligible or negative performance changes compared to human-curated Skills, indicating models struggle to reliably author effective procedural knowledge.
Skills can effectively substitute for model scale, allowing smaller LLMs augmented with Skills to achieve performance comparable to larger models without Skills, suggesting cost-effectiveness.
originalProblem: A lack of systematic evaluation for the efficacy of "Agent Skills" in augmenting large language model (LLM) agents at inference time.
Existing agent benchmarks primarily assess raw model capabilities in isolation, rather than quantifying the value added by external procedural knowledge.
Practitioners and researchers lacked empirical data to guide the adoption, design, and development of effective Agent Skills.
results: Curated Skills improved average agent performance by +16.2 percentage points across 7 model-harness configurations, with the largest absolute improvement of +23.3pp.

Skills benefit varied across domains, with Healthcare (+51.9pp) and Manufacturing (+41.9pp) showing the largest gains, while Mathematics (+6.0pp) and Software Engineering (+4.5pp) saw smaller improvements.
Optimal Skills quantity was found to be 2-3 Skills per task (+18.6pp), and moderate-length Skills provided larger benefits than comprehensive, verbose ones (which resulted in a -2.9pp delta).
solution: Developed SKILLSBENCH, a containerized evaluation framework that assesses every task under both vanilla (no Skills) and Skills-augmented conditions to directly measure efficacy.

Defined a structured "Skill" artifact as procedural content for a class of problems, distinguishing it from system prompts or few-shot examples, and comprising instructions and optional executable resources.
Constructed a diverse dataset of 84 tasks across 11 domains with human-authored instructions, emphasizing Skill generality, deterministic verification, and strict quality filtering for submissions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced SkillsBench, a benchmark to evaluate how effectively "Agent Skills" augment large language model (LLM) agents, measuring performance across diverse tasks and agent-model configurations. The benchmark found that curated Skills improved average agent performance by +16.2 percentage points and can enable smaller LLMs to match the performance of larger models operating without Skills.

title: SkillsBench: Benchmarking How Well Agent Skills Work Across Diverse Tasks

abstract: Developing autonomous LLM agents capable of making a series of intelligent decisions to solve complex, real-world tasks is a fast-evolving frontier. Like human cognitive development, agents are expected to acquire knowledge and skills through exploration and interaction with the environment. Despite advances, the community still lacks a unified, interactive reinforcement learning (RL) framework that can effectively train such agents from scratch -- without relying on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) -- across diverse and realistic environments. To bridge this gap, we introduce AgentGym-RL, a new framework to train LLM agents for multi-turn interactive decision-making through RL. The framework features a modular and decoupled architecture, ensuring high flexibility and extensibility. It encompasses a wide variety of real-world scenarios, and supports mainstream RL algorithms. Furthermore, we propose ScalingInter-RL, a training approach designed for exploration-exploitation balance and stable RL optimization. In early stages, it emphasizes exploitation by restricting the number of interactions, and gradually shifts towards exploration with larger horizons to encourage diverse problem-solving strategies. In this way, the agent develops more diverse behaviors and is less prone to collapse under long horizons. We perform extensive experiments to validate the stability and effectiveness of both the AgentGym-RL framework and the ScalingInter-RL approach. Our agents match or surpass commercial models on 27 tasks across diverse environments. We offer key insights and will open-source the complete AgentGym-RL framework -- including code and datasets -- to empower the research community in developing the next generation of intelligent agents.

keyInsights: Strategic investment in post-training and test-time computation through RL and increased interaction turns is more impactful for developing agentic intelligence than merely increasing a model's parameter count.

Progressive interaction scaling during RL training (ScalingInter-RL) is crucial for stable optimization and achieving robust performance in long-horizon tasks, effectively balancing initial exploitation with gradual exploration.

RL training yields greater performance improvements in structured environments with clear rules (e.g., TextCraft, BabyAI, SciWorld) compared to more open-ended and noisy environments like WebArena and Deep Search.
originalProblem: A lack of a unified, interactive reinforcement learning (RL) framework for training LLM agents from scratch for complex, multi-turn, long-horizon tasks.

Existing LLM agent development methods, such as prompting or supervised fine-tuning (SFT), often rely on proprietary models or human-curated data, limiting scalability, adaptability, and intrinsic self-improvement.
Traditional RL applications for LLMs are largely confined to single-turn tasks or face instability and inefficiency issues when extended to multi-turn agent decision-making.
results: An open-source 7B model, trained with AgentGym-RL and ScalingInter-RL, achieved an average success rate that matched or surpassed leading commercial, closed-source models (e.g., GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro) across five diverse scenarios.

ScalingInter-RL consistently delivered over 10% improvement on WebArena and a 30-point improvement on TextCraft compared to baseline RL approaches, demonstrating stable and sustained reward increases throughout training.

The RL-trained 7B model significantly outperformed much larger open-source models (e.g., Llama3.1-70B, Qwen2.5-72B with nearly ten times the parameters) in overall accuracy, indicating the efficacy of RL over raw model scale for agent capabilities.
solution: Introduces AgentGym-RL, a modular, open-source framework with decoupled environment, agent, and training modules to support diverse multi-turn, long-horizon tasks.

Develops ScalingInter-RL, a novel progressive interaction scaling strategy that adaptively increases the maximum number of turns during RL training to balance exploration and exploitation, improving optimization stability and efficiency.

Integrates a unified RL pipeline that supports mainstream online RL algorithms (e.g., PPO, GRPO) and includes engineering optimizations for scalability and reliability across various realistic environments.
paper_summary.summary: An open-source framework, AgentGym-RL, facilitates the training of large language model agents for long-horizon decision-making through multi-turn reinforcement learning and a progressive interaction scaling strategy called ScalingInter-RL. This approach enables a 7B parameter model to achieve an average success rate comparable to or exceeding larger proprietary models across diverse environments, highlighting the impact of RL training on agentic intelligence.

title: AgentGym-RL: Training LLM Agents for Long-Horizon Decision Making through Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Procedural memory enables large language model (LLM) agents to internalize "how-to" knowledge, theoretically reducing redundant trial-and-error. However, existing frameworks predominantly suffer from a "passive accumulation" paradigm, treating memory as a static append-only archive. To bridge the gap between static storage and dynamic reasoning, we propose $\textbf{ReMe}$ ($\textit{Remember Me, Refine Me}$), a comprehensive framework for experience-driven agent evolution. ReMe innovates across the memory lifecycle via three mechanisms: 1) $\textit{multi-faceted distillation}$, which extracts fine-grained experiences by recognizing success patterns, analyzing failure triggers and generating comparative insights; 2) $\textit{context-adaptive reuse}$, which tailors historical insights to new contexts via scenario-aware indexing; and 3) $\textit{utility-based refinement}$, which autonomously adds valid memories and prunes outdated ones to maintain a compact, high-quality experience pool. Extensive experiments on BFCL-V3 and AppWorld demonstrate that ReMe establishes a new state-of-the-art in agent memory system. Crucially, we observe a significant memory-scaling effect: Qwen3-8B equipped with ReMe outperforms larger, memoryless Qwen3-14B, suggesting that self-evolving memory provides a computation-efficient pathway for lifelong learning. We release our code and the $\texttt{this http URL}$ dataset to facilitate further research.

keyInsights: Procedural memory for LLM agents is more effective when dynamic and feedback-driven, rather than a passive, append-only archive.
The quality and granularity of distilled experiences, specifically 'keypoint-level' information, are more crucial for knowledge transfer than raw, full trajectories.
Smaller LLMs, when augmented with a sophisticated dynamic memory framework, can match or exceed the performance of significantly larger, memory-less models, demonstrating a 'memory-scaling effect'.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent procedural memory systems passively accumulate raw data, leading to coarse-grained information and a lack of adaptive application of past experiences.
The absence of dynamic update strategies causes memory pools to accumulate outdated or unhelpful information, degrading performance over time.
Agents struggle to efficiently internalize 'how-to' knowledge, resulting in redundant trial-and-error and limited continuous improvement.
results: ReMe consistently achieved state-of-the-art performance across BFCL-V3 and AppWorld benchmarks, with Qwen3-8B + ReMe (dynamic) yielding average gains of 8.83% in Avg@4 and 7.29% in Pass@4 over a 'No Memory' baseline.
The framework demonstrated a memory-scaling effect, where Qwen3-8B + ReMe (dynamic) outperformed the vanilla Qwen3-14B model, and Qwen3-14B + ReMe (dynamic) surpassed the vanilla Qwen3-32B model.

Dynamic refinement components (selective addition, failure-aware reflection, utility-based deletion) were shown to be crucial, with keypoint-level experiences and LLM-generated 'usage scenarios' proving most effective for indexing and retrieval.
solution: ReMe implements a closed-loop system of experience acquisition, reuse, and refinement for LLM agents.
Experience acquisition distills high-quality, structured 'keypoint-level' knowledge from multi-faceted analyses of both successful and failed trajectories.

Experience reuse involves retrieving relevant insights, adaptively reranking, and rewriting them to provide task-specific guidance; refinement continuously optimizes memory through selective addition, failure-aware reflection, and utility-based deletion.
paper_summary.summary: ReMe introduces a dynamic procedural memory framework for LLM agents, enabling continuous learning and adaptation through a closed-loop system of experience acquisition, reuse, and refinement. This framework allows smaller language models to achieve performance comparable to or surpassing larger, memory-less models, demonstrating a memory-scaling effect and improving agent robustness.

title: Remember Me, Refine Me: A Dynamic Procedural Memory Framework for Experience-Driven Agent Evolution

abstract: Most Large Language Model (LLM) agent memory systems rely on a small set of static, hand-designed operations for extracting memory. These fixed procedures hard-code human priors about what to store and how to revise memory, making them rigid under diverse interaction patterns and inefficient on long histories. To this end, we present \textbf{MemSkill}, which reframes these operations as learnable and evolvable memory skills, structured and reusable routines for extracting, consolidating, and pruning information from interaction traces. Inspired by the design philosophy of agent skills, MemSkill employs a \emph{controller} that learns to select a small set of relevant skills, paired with an LLM-based \emph{executor} that produces skill-guided memories. Beyond learning skill selection, MemSkill introduces a \emph{designer} that periodically reviews hard cases where selected skills yield incorrect or incomplete memories, and evolves the skill set by proposing refinements and new skills. Together, MemSkill forms a closed-loop procedure that improves both the skill-selection policy and the skill set itself. Experiments on LoCoMo, LongMemEval, HotpotQA, and ALFWorld demonstrate that MemSkill improves task performance over strong baselines and generalizes well across settings. Further analyses shed light on how skills evolve, offering insights toward more adaptive, self-evolving memory management for LLM agents.

keyInsights: Memory management for LLM agents can be conceptualized as a set of dynamic, reusable memory skills that can be learned and refined over time.
A continuous feedback loop, where an agent learns to utilize skills and a designer periodically evolves these skills based on performance, leads to self-improving memory systems.
Explicitly structuring memory behaviors into an interpretable skill bank promotes adaptability, reusability across tasks, and offers a pathway towards more autonomous LLM agents.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent memory systems rely on static, human-engineered operations, which are rigid and inefficient for long, open-ended interactions.
The hard-coded nature of memory extraction, consolidation, and pruning limits adaptability to diverse interaction patterns and hinders scalability over growing interaction histories.
Prior methods primarily optimize *how to use* a predefined set of memory operations rather than enabling the evolution of the operations themselves.
results: MemSkill consistently achieved superior performance, obtaining the highest LLM-judge scores on conversational benchmarks (LoCoMo, LongMemEval) and the highest success rates on embodied interactive tasks (ALFWorld) compared to strong baselines.

The system demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, with learned skills transferring effectively across different base LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-3 to Qwen3) and diverse datasets (e.g., LoCoMo to HotpotQA) without requiring retraining.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the learned skill selection by the controller and the skill evolution process orchestrated by the designer are critical components contributing to MemSkill's performance gains.
solution: MemSkill reframes memory operations as "learnable and evolvable memory skills" stored in a dynamic "skill bank."
It employs a closed-loop optimization recipe combining reinforcement learning for a controller to select relevant skills and an LLM-guided designer to evolve the skill bank from hard cases.
A fixed LLM executor constructs skill-guided memories for text spans by conditioning on selected skills, generating structured memory updates in a single pass.
paper_summary.summary: MemSkill introduces an approach for Large Language Model (LLM) agents where memory operations are learned and evolve over time, moving beyond static, human-engineered mechanisms. This system, developed by Nanyang Technological University and collaborators, achieves superior performance in conversational and embodied tasks by continually refining a dynamic skill bank and a skill-selection policy.

title: MemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents

abstract: The operational efficacy of large language models relies heavily on their inference-time context. This has established Context Engineering (CE) as a formal discipline for optimizing these inputs. Current CE methods rely on manually crafted harnesses, such as rigid generation-reflection workflows and predefined context schemas. They impose structural biases and restrict context optimization to a narrow, intuition-bound design space. To address this, we introduce Meta Context Engineering (MCE), a bi-level framework that supersedes static CE heuristics by co-evolving CE skills and context artifacts. In MCE iterations, a meta-level agent refines engineering skills via agentic crossover, a deliberative search over the history of skills, their executions, and evaluations. A base-level agent executes these skills, learns from training rollouts, and optimizes context as flexible files and code. We evaluate MCE across five disparate domains under offline and online settings. MCE demonstrates consistent performance gains, achieving 5.6--53.8% relative improvement over state-of-the-art agentic CE methods (mean of 16.9%), while maintaining superior context adaptability, transferability, and efficiency in both context usage and training.

keyInsights: Context Engineering can be formulated as a bi-level optimization problem, allowing for the co-evolution of context generation strategies (skills) and the resulting context artifacts.
"Agentic crossover" provides a flexible and deliberative mechanism for a meta-level agent to synthesize superior context engineering skills by analyzing past performance and task requirements.
Representing context as arbitrary files and code, rather than rigid schemas, enables fully agentic context optimization at the base-level, free from structural biases.
originalProblem: Existing Context Engineering (CE) methods rely on manually crafted "agentic harnesses" that introduce structural biases and rigid context representations.
Current approaches constrain context optimization to an "intuition-bound design space," limiting the discovery of truly task-optimal strategies.
Static CE heuristics result in contexts that lack adaptability, token efficiency, and transferability across various LLM capacities and task complexities.
results: MCE consistently outperformed state-of-the-art context engineering methods, achieving an average relative improvement of 89.1% over base models in offline settings and 74.1% in online settings.
The framework demonstrated superior context efficiency, achieving higher performance with significantly fewer tokens (e.g., 75% accuracy with 20K tokens on FiNER vs. 70% with 79K for ACE).
MCE substantially reduced training duration by 13.6x and required 4.8x fewer rollouts compared to leading baselines, while also enhancing context transferability between LLMs.
solution: MCE introduces a bi-level optimization framework that enables the co-evolution of Context Engineering (CE) "skills" (strategies) and context "artifacts."
A meta-level agent iteratively refines CE skills using "agentic crossover," synthesizing superior strategies by reasoning over historical data.
A base-level agent executes these evolved skills to construct and optimize context, represented as flexible files and code, using coding toolkits and file system access.
paper_summary.summary: Meta Context Engineering (MCE) enables large language model agents to autonomously co-evolve their context engineering strategies (skills) and context artifacts through a bi-level optimization framework. This method enhances performance and efficiency by dynamically adapting context properties, yielding an average relative improvement of 89.1% over base models and outperforming state-of-the-art context engineering methods.

title: Meta Context Engineering via Agentic Skill Evolution

abstract: Coding agents are increasingly used as general-purpose problem solvers, but their flexibility does not by itself confer the domain expertise needed for specialized tasks. Recent work addresses this through \textit{agent skills}: reusable workflows, and code, that augment agents with domain-specific capabilities. Most skills today are hand-crafted, and existing evolutionary approaches optimize low-level artifacts (e.g. prompts \& code) that are tightly coupled to specific models and tasks. We introduce \textbf{EvoSkill}, a self-evolving framework that automatically discovers and refines agent skills through iterative failure analysis. EvoSkill analyzes execution failures, proposes new skills or edits to existing ones, and materializes them into structured, reusable skill folders. A Pareto frontier of agent programs governs selection, retaining only skills that improve held-out validation performance while the underlying model remains frozen. We evaluate EvoSkill on two benchmarks: OfficeQA, a grounded reasoning benchmark over U.S.\ Treasury data, where it improves exact-match accuracy by \textbf{7.3\%} (60.6\% $\to$ 67.9\%); and SealQA, a search-augmented QA benchmark with noisy retrieval, where it yields a \textbf{12.1\%} gain (26.6\% $\to$ 38.7\%). We also investigate the zero-shot transfer capabilties of skills evolved on one task to the other; in particular: skills evolved from SealQA transfers zero-shot to BrowseComp, improving accuracy by \textbf{5.3\%} without modification demonstrating that skill-level optimization produces transferable capabilities beyond the training task.

keyInsights: Iterative, failure-driven textual feedback from a specialized LLM-based Proposer can effectively guide the automated discovery and refinement of agent skills, leading to continuous performance improvements.

Optimizing agent capabilities at the "skill" abstraction level yields more interpretable, composable, and crucially, transferable knowledge compared to optimizing lower-level artifacts like prompts or raw code.
Skills discovered in independent evolutionary runs can be complementary, and their aggregation into a combined library leads to a more comprehensive and robust set of agent capabilities.
originalProblem: Coding agents, despite their general flexibility, often lack specialized domain expertise for consistently high performance on complex tasks.
Manually crafting domain-specific skills for agents is resource-intensive, requires significant human expertise, and does not scale effectively with increasing task complexity.

Existing automated methods for agent improvement, such as optimizing low-level prompts or code, result in artifacts that are tightly coupled to specific models and tasks, limiting reusability and transferability.
results: On the OfficeQA benchmark, EvoSkill consistently improved exact-match accuracy over the baseline, with a skill-merge configuration achieving a substantial +7.3 percentage point gain (from 60.6% to 67.9%).
For the SealQA benchmark, EvoSkill significantly enhanced accuracy by +12.1 percentage points (from 26.6% to 38.7%), demonstrating its effectiveness on challenging search-intensive tasks.

A "search-persistence-protocol" skill, evolved on SealQA, successfully transferred zero-shot to the unseen BrowseComp benchmark, yielding a +5.3 percentage point accuracy improvement (from 43.5% to 48.8%).
solution: EvoSkill proposes a self-evolving framework utilizing three collaborating LLM-based agents: an Executor (runs tasks), a Proposer (diagnoses failures and generates high-level skill proposals), and a Skill-Builder (materializes proposals into concrete, structured skills).

The framework operates within an iterative loop that selects agent programs, collects failures, generates skill proposals based on textual feedback and historical data, builds candidate skills, and updates a Pareto frontier of successful programs.

Skills are optimized at a higher level of abstraction, focusing on discovering and refining structured, reusable skill modules with trigger conditions and procedural instructions, rather than directly modifying low-level prompts or code.
paper_summary.summary: EvoSkill introduces a self-evolving framework that autonomously discovers and refines reusable agent skills by analyzing execution failures and leveraging textual feedback. The system demonstrated consistent performance improvements on challenging benchmarks like OfficeQA (up to +7.3 percentage points exact-match accuracy) and SealQA (+12.1 percentage points accuracy), and showed zero-shot skill transferability to unseen tasks.

title: EvoSkill: Automated Skill Discovery for Multi-Agent Systems

abstract: Compound AI systems promise capabilities beyond those of individual models, yet their success depends critically on effective orchestration. Existing routing approaches face two limitations: (1) input-level routers make coarse query-level decisions that ignore evolving task requirements; (2) RL-trained orchestrators are expensive to adapt and often suffer from routing collapse, repeatedly invoking one strong but costly option in multi-turn scenarios. We introduce SkillOrchestra, a framework for skill-aware orchestration. Instead of directly learning a routing policy end-to-end, SkillOrchestra learns fine-grained skills from execution experience and models agent-specific competence and cost under those skills. At deployment, the orchestrator infers the skill demands of the current interaction and selects agents that best satisfy them under an explicit performance-cost trade-off. Extensive experiments across ten benchmarks demonstrate that SkillOrchestra outperforms SoTA RL-based orchestrators by up to 22.5% with 700x and 300x learning cost reduction compared to Router-R1 and ToolOrchestra, respectively. These results show that explicit skill modeling enables scalable, interpretable, and sample-efficient orchestration, offering a principled alternative to data-intensive RL-based approaches. The code is available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling abstract skills and agent profiles in a structured "Skill Handbook" decouples orchestration knowledge from orchestrator parameters, enabling greater modularity and transferability.
An LLM-based skill discovery and refinement mechanism can automatically extract and organize fine-grained, reusable capabilities from execution traces.
Orchestration via a learned skill handbook and explicit performance-cost optimization mitigates the "routing collapse" issue, leading to more balanced and efficient agent utilization.
originalProblem: Existing model routers make single-shot, input-level decisions, failing to handle dynamic, multi-turn interactions and evolving task requirements in compound AI systems.

Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based orchestrators suffer from high training costs, poor adaptability to new models/tools, and "routing collapse" where they over-rely on a single powerful agent, leading to inflated costs and suboptimal resource use.
Lack of a data-efficient, transferable, and stable orchestration framework that can effectively utilize diverse AI agents while optimizing performance-cost trade-offs.
results: SkillOrchestra achieves superior effectiveness, outperforming RL-based Router-R1 by 10.0 EM points on QA tasks and ToolOrchestra by 8.0 percentage points in accuracy on the FRAMES agent orchestration benchmark.
It demonstrates enhanced efficiency and Pareto optimality, achieving higher accuracy at approximately 2x lower inference costs and 300x to 700x lower training costs compared to RL baselines.

The framework mitigates routing collapse, exhibiting balanced agent utilization by distributing calls across diverse models (e.g., Mixtral-8x22B 44.53%, Qwen2.5-7B 25.99%), and transfers orchestration knowledge effectively across different orchestrator backbones.
solution: SkillOrchestra reframes orchestration as skill-grounded decision-making, introducing abstract "skills" that define task requirements and agent capabilities.
It constructs a reusable "Skill Handbook" through an LLM-based discovery and refinement process, detailing mode-level insights, skill definitions, and agent-specific performance/cost profiles.

At inference, an orchestrator uses the handbook, dynamically selecting operational modes and agents based on explicit competence-aware performance-cost trade-offs and relevant skills for the current state.
paper_summary.summary: SkillOrchestra introduces a framework that orchestrates AI agents by explicitly learning and leveraging a "Skill Handbook" of capabilities and costs, moving beyond implicit policy optimization. This approach yields higher accuracy at lower costs, achieving up to a 10.0 exact match point improvement on QA tasks and an 8.0 percentage point increase in accuracy on complex agent orchestration benchmarks, while significantly reducing training and inference expenses by hundreds of times.

title: SkillOrchestra: Learning to Route Agents via Skill Transfer

abstract: Equipping Large Language Model (LLM) agents with domain-specific skills is critical for tackling complex tasks. Yet, manual authoring creates a severe scalability bottleneck. Conversely, automated skill generation often yields fragile or fragmented results because it either relies on shallow parametric knowledge or sequentially overfits to non-generalizable trajectory-local lessons. To overcome this, we introduce Trace2Skill, a framework that mirrors how human experts author skills: by holistically analyzing broad execution experience before distilling it into a single, comprehensive guide. Instead of reacting sequentially to individual trajectories, Trace2Skill dispatches a parallel fleet of sub-agents to analyze a diverse pool of executions. It extracts trajectory-specific lessons and hierarchically consolidates them into a unified, conflict-free skill directory via inductive reasoning. Trace2Skill supports both deepening existing human-written skills and creating new ones from scratch. Experiments in challenging domains, such as spreadsheet, VisionQA and math reasoning, show that Trace2Skill significantly improves upon strong baselines, including Anthropic's official xlsx skills. Crucially, this trajectory-grounded evolution does not merely memorize task instances or model-specific quirks: evolved skills transfer across LLM scales and generalize to OOD settings. For example, skills evolved by Qwen3.5-35B on its own trajectories improved a Qwen3.5-122B agent by up to 57.65 absolute percentage points on WikiTableQuestions. Ultimately, our results demonstrate that complex agent experience can be packaged into highly transferable, declarative skills -- requiring no parameter updates, no external retrieval modules, and utilizing open-source models as small as 35B parameters.

keyInsights: Agent experience can be distilled into transferable, declarative skills that generalize across LLM scales and out-of-distribution tasks, challenging the assumption that experience is inherently model- and task-specific.
Parallel, holistic consolidation of execution trajectories significantly outperforms sequential online editing and retrieval-based memory systems in both skill quality and computational efficiency.

Robust skill evolution, including both deepening existing human skills and creating new ones from scratch, is achievable using smaller, open-source LLMs (e.g., 35B parameters), reducing dependency on proprietary models.
originalProblem: Manually authoring and maintaining high-quality, domain-specific skills for LLM agents is resource-intensive and does not scale well.
Existing automated skill generation methods often lead to fragmented skill collections, premature overfitting to local lessons, and difficulties with retrieval-based memory systems.
Current automated approaches for skill evolution differ from how human experts consolidate broad understanding into comprehensive, generalized skill documents.
results: Skills evolved by a Qwen3.5-35B model improved a Qwen3.5-122B agent by up to 57.65 absolute percentage points on WikiTableQuestions, demonstrating strong cross-model and out-of-distribution transferability.

Trace2Skill reliably strengthened human-written skills (e.g., +21.5 pp on SpreadsheetBench-Verified) and substantially outperformed parametric baselines for skill creation, often matching or exceeding human-written quality.

Parallel consolidation was approximately 20 times faster than sequential updates and outperformed a ReasoningBank-style retrieval baseline (e.g., +13.8 pp on SpreadsheetBench-Verified) by inductive compression and active deduplication.
solution: Trace2Skill employs a three-stage pipeline (Trajectory Generation, Parallel Multi-Agent Patch Proposal, Conflict-Free Patch Consolidation) to process diverse agent execution experiences.
A fleet of specialized sub-agents (Success Analysts and Error Analysts) independently proposes skill patches from successful and failing trajectories in parallel.

A hierarchical merging process inductively consolidates proposed patches into a single, comprehensive, conflict-free skill document, prioritizing generalizable patterns and resolving conflicts programmatically.
paper_summary.summary: The Trace2Skill framework enables the distillation of LLM agent execution trajectories into transferable, declarative skills, converting diverse agent experiences into comprehensive guides. This approach leads to performance improvements of up to 57.65 absolute percentage points on tasks like WikiTableQuestions and demonstrates effective generalization across different model scales and out-of-distribution task domains.

title: Trace2Skill: Distill Trajectory-Local Lessons into Transferable Agent Skills

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in complex reasoning and multi-turn interactions but struggle to continuously improve and adapt when deployed in new environments. One promising approach is implementing skill libraries that allow agents to learn, validate, and apply new skills. However, current skill library approaches rely primarily on LLM prompting, making consistent skill library implementation challenging. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based approach to enhance agents' self-improvement capabilities with a skill library. Specifically, we introduce Skill Augmented GRPO for self-Evolution (SAGE), a novel RL framework that systematically incorporates skills into learning. The framework's key component, Sequential Rollout, iteratively deploys agents across a chain of similar tasks for each rollout. As agents navigate through the task chain, skills generated from previous tasks accumulate in the library and become available for subsequent tasks. Additionally, the framework enhances skill generation and utilization through a Skill-integrated Reward that complements the original outcome-based rewards. Experimental results on AppWorld demonstrate that SAGE, when applied to supervised-finetuned model with expert experience, achieves 8.9% higher Scenario Goal Completion while requiring 26% fewer interaction steps and generating 59% fewer tokens, substantially outperforming existing approaches in both accuracy and efficiency.

keyInsights: Explicitly rewarding skill generation and usage within an RL framework (Skill-integrated Reward) significantly enhances the adoption and effectiveness of learned skills in LLM agents.
Training agents on sequential tasks (Sequential Rollout) enables the validation of skill quality over multiple interactions, providing better feedback for skill learning.

Supervised fine-tuning with high-quality expert demonstrations is crucial for initializing LLM agents to effectively follow the skill library agent format and achieve optimal performance with subsequent RL training.
originalProblem: LLM-based agents struggle with continuous improvement and adaptation to new environments after deployment, limiting their effectiveness in dynamic scenarios.
Existing skill library implementations rely on manual LLM prompting, resulting in inconsistent skill quality and limited adaptability tied to the base model's instruction-following capabilities.
Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) approaches typically focus on individual task outcomes, lacking explicit mechanisms to foster the development and effective utilization of reusable skills.
results: SAGE achieved 72.0% Task Goal Completion (TGC) and 60.7% Scenario Goal Completion (SGC) on the AppWorld Test Normal dataset, representing an 8.9% increase in SGC over the baseline GRPO method.
The framework improved efficiency by requiring 26% fewer average interaction steps (12.1 vs. 16.4) and generating 59% fewer tokens (1,475 vs. 3,613) compared to the baseline GRPO.

SAGE-trained open-source models (Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct) surpassed the performance of the proprietary expert model (Claude Sonnet 3.5 V2 ReAct) that was used to generate the supervised fine-tuning data.
solution: Introduced Skill Augmented GRPO for self-Evolution (SAGE), a novel reinforcement learning framework that systematically incorporates skill learning into LLM agent training.

Developed a specialized skill library agent that unifies task solving and skill generation, allowing agents to generate, use, update, and save skill functions (programmatic actions) during task execution.

Extended the GRPO algorithm with 'Sequential Rollout' (training on chains of similar tasks to allow skill feedback across tasks) and 'Skill-integrated Reward' (providing explicit bonuses for successful skill generation and utilization).
paper_summary.summary: The SAGE reinforcement learning framework, developed by a team from the University of Wisconsin–Madison and AWS Agentic AI, systematically integrates skill learning into LLM agent training. This framework enhances agent self-improvement by enabling dynamic skill generation and utilization, leading to an 8.9% higher scenario goal completion, 26% fewer interaction steps, and 59% fewer generated tokens on the AppWorld dataset compared to baseline methods.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Self-Improving Agent with Skill Library

abstract: Reusable skills let LLM agents package task-specific procedures, tool affordances, and execution guidance into modular building blocks. As skill ecosystems grow to tens of thousands of entries, exposing every skill at inference time becomes infeasible. This creates a skill-routing problem: given a user task, the system must identify relevant skills before downstream planning or execution. Existing agent stacks often rely on progressive disclosure, exposing only skill names and descriptions while hiding the full implementation body. We examine this design choice on a SkillsBench-derived benchmark with approximately 80K candidate skills, targeting the practically important setting of large skill registries with heavy overlap. Across representative sparse, dense, and reranking baselines on this setting, hiding the skill body causes a 31--44 percentage point drop in routing accuracy, showing that full skill text is a critical routing signal in this setting rather than a minor metadata refinement. Motivated by this finding, we present SkillRouter, a compact 1.2B full-text retrieve-and-rerank pipeline. SkillRouter achieves 74.0% Hit@1 on our benchmark -- the strongest average top-1 routing performance among the baselines we evaluate -- while using 13$\times$ fewer parameters and running 5.8$\times$ faster than the strongest base pipeline. The ranking gains further generalize to a supplementary benchmark independently constructed from three skill sources. In a complementary end-to-end study across four coding agents, routing gains transfer to improved task success, with larger gains for more capable agents.

keyInsights: Skill routing in large-scale skill pools is a genuinely challenging retrieval problem, with even strong 8B base encoders achieving only 64.0% Hit@1 accuracy.

The full skill implementation body is the decisive signal for accurate skill selection, causing a 29-44 percentage point degradation in Hit@1 when excluded and commanding over 91% of cross-encoder attention.

A compact, fine-tuned retrieve-and-rerank pipeline leveraging the full skill body, coupled with listwise loss for reranking, can effectively address the challenge and achieve high accuracy on consumer-grade hardware.
originalProblem: LLM agents face a challenge in selecting relevant skills from vast and growing repositories (tens of thousands) due to limited context window size.
Community skill repositories exhibit pervasive functional overlap, where skills with similar names/descriptions hide critical differences in their implementation details.
Existing agent architectures often assume skill names and descriptions are sufficient for selection, a premise that has not been systematically verified for large-scale skill pools.
results: The compact SKILLROUTER-0.6B pipeline (1.2B parameters total) achieved 74.0% average Hit@1 accuracy, representing an 8.6 percentage point improvement over its encoder-only variant (SR-Emb-0.6B).
Removing the skill body from input caused a significant drop in Hit@1 accuracy (e.g., 36.0 percentage points for SR-Emb-0.6B), confirming its critical role in skill selection.

The fine-tuned SR-Emb-0.6B encoder outperformed larger zero-shot models, including OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large, while the reranker's listwise cross-entropy loss yielded a 30.7 percentage point gain over pointwise loss.
solution: Developed SKILLROUTER, a compact two-stage retrieve-and-rerank pipeline consisting of a 0.6B-parameter bi-encoder (SR-Emb-0.6B) and a 0.6B-parameter cross-encoder (SR-Rank-0.6B).
Constructed a large-scale benchmark with approximately 80,000 skills and 75 expert-curated task queries, including a 'Hard' tier with LLM-generated distractors.
Employed a fine-tuning strategy for both stages, utilizing GPT-4o-mini to generate synthetic training data, incorporating hard negative mining and a three-layer false negative filtering pipeline.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Alibaba Group developed SkillRouter, a two-stage retrieve-and-rerank pipeline for efficient and accurate skill selection in LLM agents from pools of tens of thousands of skills. This compact 1.2 billion-parameter system achieved 74.0% Hit@1 accuracy and empirically established that the full skill implementation body is crucial for effective routing, rather than just metadata.

title: SkillRouter: Skill Routing for LLM Agents at Scale

abstract: In practical LLM applications, users repeatedly express stable preferences and requirements, such as reducing hallucinations, following institutional writing conventions, or avoiding overly technical wording, yet such interaction experience is seldom consolidated into reusable knowledge. Consequently, LLM agents often fail to accumulate personalized capabilities across sessions. We present AutoSkill, an experience-driven lifelong learning framework that enables LLM agents to automatically derive, maintain, and reuse skills from dialogue and interaction traces. AutoSkill abstracts skills from user experience, supports their continual self-evolution, and dynamically injects relevant skills into future requests without retraining the underlying model. Designed as a model-agnostic plugin layer, it is compatible with existing LLMs and introduces a standardized skill representation for sharing and transfer across agents, users, and tasks. In this way, AutoSkill turns ephemeral interaction experience into explicit, reusable, and composable capabilities. This paper describes the motivation, architecture, skill lifecycle, and implementation of AutoSkill, and positions it with respect to prior work on memory, retrieval, personalization, and agentic systems. AutoSkill highlights a practical and scalable path toward lifelong personalized agents and personal digital surrogates.

keyInsights: Ephemeral user interactions can be abstracted into explicit, versioned, and editable skill artifacts for lifelong learning.
A training-free, prompt-driven modular architecture facilitates continuous skill evolution and reuse without modifying base LLM parameters.
Hybrid retrieval and a dedicated LLM-based judge allow for effective skill discovery, refinement through merging, and management.
originalProblem: LLM agents struggle to consolidate stable user preferences and operational requirements into reusable capabilities across interaction sessions.
Current approaches, like memory systems or implicit skill learning, often lack transparency, controllability, and efficient personalization.
Users must repeatedly provide the same instructions, as agents fail to accumulate personalized knowledge over time.
results: The framework extracted 1858 skills from 22,611 real user conversations within the WildChat-1M corpus.
Skill categories were diverse, predominantly featuring "Programming & Software Dev.", "Writing & Content Creation", and "Data & AI/ML" tasks.
Version tracking confirmed iterative refinement, with one English skill observed at version 0.1.34, demonstrating continuous optimization.
Case studies showed the framework successfully abstracts both soft conversational styles and rigid task-execution procedures into explicit, editable skill artifacts.
solution: An experience-driven, training-free lifelong learning framework for LLM agents that externalizes reusable task-solving patterns as versioned skills.
Employs two loops: an online path for skill-enhanced response generation and a background path for real-time skill evolution.
Skills are represented as explicit, structured `SKILL.md` artifacts, enabling transparency, human oversight, and version control.
paper_summary.summary: AutoSkill is a training-free framework enabling Large Language Model agents to acquire and refine explicit, versioned skills from user interactions. This allows agents to continuously adapt to personalized preferences and operational requirements without modifying base model parameters.

title: AutoSkill: Experience-Driven Lifelong Learning via Skill Self-Evolution

abstract: Anthropic proposes the concept of skills for LLM agents to tackle multi-step professional tasks that simple tool invocations cannot address. A tool is a single, self-contained function, whereas a skill is a structured bundle of interdependent multi-file artifacts. Currently, skill generation is not only label-intensive due to manual authoring, but also may suffer from human--machine cognitive misalignment, which can lead to degraded agent performance, as evidenced by evaluations on SkillsBench. Therefore, we aim to enable agents to autonomously generate skills. However, existing self-evolving methods designed for tools cannot be directly applied to skills due to their increased complexity. To address these issues, we propose EvoSkills, a self-evolving skills framework that enables agents to autonomously construct complex, multi-file skill packages. Specifically, EvoSkills couples a Skill Generator that iteratively refines skills with a Surrogate Verifier that co-evolves to provide informative and actionable feedback without access to ground-truth test content. On SkillsBench, EvoSkills achieves the highest pass rate among five baselines on both Claude Code and Codex, and also exhibits strong generalization capabilities to six additional LLMs.

keyInsights: LLM agents can autonomously generate more effective and reliable skills than human experts, as these self-evolved skills better align with the agent's intrinsic reasoning and execution patterns.
A co-evolutionary framework, especially with an information-isolated surrogate verifier, provides crucial, actionable feedback for skill refinement without requiring direct ground-truth supervision.
Self-evolved skills demonstrate strong cross-model transferability, suggesting they encode reusable task structures rather than model-specific artifacts, making them broadly portable.
originalProblem: Manual human authoring of 'agent skills' is labor-intensive, difficult to scale, and often leads to inconsistent performance due to 'human-machine cognitive misalignment'.
Existing self-evolving methods for LLM agents focus primarily on atomic tools or function APIs, lacking the capability to generate and refine complex, structured, multi-file skill packages.
Many self-evolution approaches require ground-truth supervision for failure diagnosis, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios where such detailed feedback is unavailable.
results: EvoSkills achieved a 71.1% pass rate on SkillsBench with Claude Opus 4.6, outperforming the no-skill baseline (30.6%) by +40.5 percentage points and human-curated skills (53.5%) by +17.6 percentage points.
Ablation studies confirmed that removing the surrogate verifier led to a substantial drop in pass rate by -30.0 percentage points, highlighting its critical role in targeted repairs.
Skills evolved by Claude Opus 4.6 showed strong transferability across six other LLM models, improving their respective no-skill baselines by +36 to +44 percentage points.

Self-evolved skills outperformed human-curated skills in 9 out of 11 professional domains, including significant gains in Finance (+56.9 pp) and Cybersecurity (+23.2 pp), and corrected degraded performance in Natural Science.
solution: The EvoSkills framework introduces a co-evolutionary approach, employing a Skill Generator and an information-isolated Surrogate Verifier that iteratively refine skills and their verification mechanisms.
The Skill Generator autonomously generates and refines multi-file skill bundles by processing task instructions and structured failure diagnostics from the verifier.

The Surrogate Verifier independently generates and refines a suite of deterministic test assertions, providing dense, actionable feedback and escalating its tests when ground-truth failures occur despite surrogate success.
paper_summary.summary: EvoSkills enables large language model agents to autonomously generate and iteratively refine complex, multi-file skill packages through a co-evolutionary verification framework. This approach yields skills that surpass human-authored versions and exhibit strong transferability across different LLM architectures, effectively bridging the cognitive gap between human-designed workflows and agent execution.

title: EvoSkills: Self-Evolving Agent Skills via Co-Evolutionary Verification

abstract: The performance of LLM-based agents depends not only on the agent itself but also on the quality of the tool interfaces it consumes. While prior work has focused heavily on agent fine-tuning, tool interfaces-including natural language descriptions and parameter schemas-remain largely human-oriented and often become a bottleneck, especially when agents must select from large candidate tool sets. Existing approaches to improving tool interfaces rely on execution traces, which are frequently unavailable in cold-start or privacy-constrained settings, and typically optimize each tool independently, limiting scalability and generalization to unseen tools. We propose Trace-Free+, a curriculum learning framework that progressively transfers supervision from trace-rich settings to trace-free deployment, encouraging the model to abstract reusable interface-usage patterns and tool usage outcomes. To support this approach, we construct a large-scale dataset of high-quality tool interfaces using a structured workflow over a diverse collection of tools. Experiments on StableToolBench and RestBench show consistent gains on unseen tools, strong cross-domain generalization, and robustness as the number of candidate tools scales to over 100, demonstrating that tool interface optimization is a practical and deployable complement to agent fine-tuning.

keyInsights: Optimizing tool interfaces by learning to rewrite descriptions, rather than solely focusing on agent fine-tuning, is a practical and deployable complement to enhance LLM-agent reliability and performance.
A curriculum learning strategy can effectively bridge the gap between trace-rich training and trace-free deployment, allowing models to abstract reusable interface-usage patterns for unseen tools.

High-quality, contextually enhanced tool descriptions significantly improve LLM-agent performance in multi-hop reasoning tasks and demonstrate strong cross-domain generalization and robustness when scaling candidate tools.
originalProblem: LLM agents struggle with suboptimal, human-oriented natural language descriptions and parameter schemas for tools, hindering effective selection and usage from large, diverse sets.
Existing methods for improving tool interfaces heavily rely on collecting tool execution traces, which is impractical in cold-start, safety-critical, or privacy-constrained deployment scenarios.

Current techniques often optimize individual tool descriptions in isolation, limiting generalizability to unseen tools and facing scalability issues with high inference costs for large-scale deployments.
results: Trace-Free+ achieved 70.1% Subtask-Level (SL) and 54.0% Query-Level (QL) success rates on StableToolBench, outperforming baseline original descriptions (D0) which scored 67.3% SL and 48.0% QL in trace-free settings.
The framework demonstrated strong cross-domain generalizability, improving the QL success rate on RestBench's TMDB dataset from 49.5% (D0) to 74.9% and on Spotify from 64.2% (D0) to 76.5%.
Trace-Free+ proved most robust in tool scaling experiments, exhibiting the least degradation in query-level success rate when the number of candidate tools was increased to over 100.
solution: The Trace-Free+ framework constructs a large-scale, high-quality supervised fine-tuning dataset by systematically improving real-world API descriptions using an agentic workflow for schema correction and trace-informed refinements.

A curriculum learning strategy is employed to fine-tune open-weight LLMs, gradually reducing the reliance on trace information during training to enable the model to generate effective tool descriptions in trace-free deployment environments.

The approach distinguishes between 'trace-based' descriptions (D2, used as ground truth) derived from execution traces and 'trace-free' generation, training the model to abstract generalizable patterns.
paper_summary.summary: Intuit AI Research developed Trace-Free+, a curriculum learning framework that generates high-quality tool descriptions for LLM-agents without needing execution traces at deployment, achieving a 74.9% query-level success rate on the TMDB RestBench dataset and maintaining robust performance with over 100 candidate tools.

title: Learning to Rewrite Tool Descriptions for Reliable LLM-Agent Tool Use

abstract: Current Large Language Model (LLM) agents show strong performance in tool use, but lack the crucial capability to systematically learn from their own experiences. While existing frameworks mainly focus on mitigating external knowledge gaps, they fail to address a more fundamental limitation: the inability to iteratively refine problem-solving strategies. In this work, we introduce EvolveR, a framework designed to enable agent to self-improve through a complete, closed-loop experience lifecycle. This lifecycle comprises two key stages: (1) Offline Self-Distillation, where the agent's interaction trajectories are synthesized into a structured repository of abstract, reusable strategic principles; (2) Online Interaction, where the agent interacts with tasks and actively retrieves distilled principles to guide its decision-making, accumulating a diverse set of behavioral trajectories. This loop employs a policy reinforcement mechanism to iteratively update the agent based on its performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of EvolveR on complex multi-hop question-answering benchmarks, where it achieves superior performance over strong agentic baselines. Our work presents a comprehensive blueprint for agents that learn not only from external data but also from the consequences of their own actions, paving the way for more autonomous and continuously improving systems. Code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Principles self-distilled by the agent's own policy model are more effective for larger models due to better "cognitive alignment" than those provided by external teacher models.

Experience retrieval from the dynamically curated experience base is an indispensable component, confirming that agents learn not just to generate good trajectories, but how to effectively apply their accumulated wisdom.

Direct internalization of principles into model parameters without a dynamic quality filter for retrieved principles can degrade performance, emphasizing the importance of the explicit, curated experience base.
originalProblem: LLM agents suffer from "operational amnesia," failing to systematically learn from their own experiences and iteratively refine problem-solving strategies.
Existing approaches either discard experiences, store raw un-distilled trajectories, or rely on external teacher models, rather than allowing agents to autonomously distill principles.
Current LLM agents lack a self-contained lifecycle for continuous self-improvement, treating each task as an isolated episode.
results: EVOLVER achieved the highest average Exact Match (EM) score of 0.382 across seven diverse question-answering benchmarks using the Qwen2.5-3B model.
The framework consistently outperformed a range of strong baselines, including advanced prompting, SFT, and state-of-the-art RL agents, demonstrating robust generalizability.

Performance scaled positively with model size, improving monotonically from 0.150 for the 0.5B model to 0.382 for the 3B model, and self-distillation by the 3B model outperformed external teacher distillation (0.382 vs. 0.370 avg. EM).
solution: EVOLVER introduces a closed-loop experience lifecycle comprising Offline Experience Self-Distillation, Online Interaction, and Policy Evolution.

Agents self-distill raw interaction trajectories into abstract strategic principles (guiding or cautionary) using their own policy model, storing them in a dynamic, quality-controlled experience base.

Policy evolution leverages Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a composite reward function to update the agent's policy, learning to effectively utilize distilled wisdom from its experiences.
paper_summary.summary: EVOLVER enables large language model agents to autonomously learn and improve from their own experiences by distilling raw interaction trajectories into strategic principles. This framework achieves an average Exact Match score of 0.382 across seven complex question-answering benchmarks, surpassing various state-of-the-art baselines.

title: EvolveR: Self-Evolving LLM Agents through an Experience-Driven Lifecycle

abstract: Current AI agents can flexibly invoke tools and execute complex tasks, yet their long-term advancement is hindered by the lack of systematic accumulation and transfer of skills. Without a unified mechanism for skill consolidation, agents frequently ``reinvent the wheel'', rediscovering solutions in isolated contexts without leveraging prior strategies. To overcome this limitation, we introduce SkillNet, an open infrastructure designed to create, evaluate, and organize AI skills at scale. SkillNet structures skills within a unified ontology that supports creating skills from heterogeneous sources, establishing rich relational connections, and performing multi-dimensional evaluation across Safety, Completeness, Executability, Maintainability, and Cost-awareness. Our infrastructure integrates a repository of over 200,000 skills, an interactive platform, and a versatile Python toolkit. Experimental evaluations on ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld demonstrate that SkillNet significantly enhances agent performance, improving average rewards by 40% and reducing execution steps by 30% across multiple backbone models. By formalizing skills as evolving, composable assets, SkillNet provides a robust foundation for agents to move from transient experience to durable mastery.

organization_info.name: Hangzhou Institute for Advanced Study, UCAS

keyInsights: Formalizing skills as structured, evaluable, and composable assets is crucial for enabling AI agents to achieve cumulative, transferable mastery over episodic learning.
A comprehensive, multi-dimensional evaluation framework is essential to ensure the reliability and quality of skills within large-scale agent repositories, mitigating "skill pollution."

Organizing skills within an ontology that models rich inter-skill relationships (e.g., 'compose_with', 'depend_on') facilitates global reasoning, efficient retrieval, and robust planning for complex agent tasks.
originalProblem: AI agents lack a systematic mechanism to accumulate and transfer skills from episodic experiences, leading to inefficient knowledge reuse.
Current agentic systems rely on transient in-context learning or manual engineering, causing agents to frequently "reinvent the wheel" for similar tasks.
There is an absence of a principled framework for validating and maintaining AI skills at scale, making existing repositories prone to unreliable or unsafe content.
results: SkillNet improved the average task reward by 40% and reduced execution steps by 30% compared to the ReAct baseline across ALFWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld environments.
Performance gains were robust across various LLM backbone models, including DeepSeek V3.2, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and o4 Mini, demonstrating complementary capabilities beyond parametric knowledge.

The project releases a large-scale open-source ecosystem, including over 200,000 skills, an interactive platform, and a Python toolkit, to foster community contribution and accelerate AI agent development.
solution: SkillNet introduces an open infrastructure for creating, evaluating, and organizing AI skills through a comprehensive, three-layered Skill Ontology (taxonomy, relation graph, package library).
An automated pipeline abstracts skills from heterogeneous sources like execution trajectories, GitHub repositories, and documents, driven by Large Language Models.

A multi-dimensional evaluation framework rigorously assesses skill quality based on Safety, Completeness, Executability, Maintainability, and Cost-awareness, with automated LLM-based evaluation showing high consistency with human judgment.
paper_summary.summary: SkillNet, an open infrastructure from Zhejiang University and collaborators, provides a unified framework for creating, evaluating, and organizing AI skills. It transitions AI agents from relying on episodic experiences to achieving durable, transferable mastery, leading to an average 40% increase in task reward and a 30% reduction in execution steps across various environments and LLM backbones.

title: SkillNet: Create, Evaluate, and Connect AI Skills

abstract: Real-world tool-using agents operate over long-horizon workflows with recurring structure and diverse demands, where effective behavior requires not only invoking atomic tools but also abstracting, and reusing higher-level tool compositions. However, existing benchmarks mainly measure instance-level success under static tool sets, offering limited insight into agents' ability to acquire such reusable skills. We address this gap by introducing SkillCraft, a benchmark explicitly stress-test agent ability to form and reuse higher-level tool compositions, where we call Skills. SkillCraft features realistic, highly compositional tool-use scenarios with difficulty scaled along both quantitative and structural dimensions, designed to elicit skill abstraction and cross-task reuse. We further propose a lightweight evaluation protocol that enables agents to auto-compose atomic tools into executable Skills, cache and reuse them inside and across tasks, thereby improving efficiency while accumulating a persistent library of reusable skills. Evaluating state-of-the-art agents on SkillCraft, we observe substantial efficiency gains, with token usage reduced by up to 80% by skill saving and reuse. Moreover, success rate strongly correlates with tool composition ability at test time, underscoring compositional skill acquisition as a core capability.

keyInsights: Compositional skill acquisition substantially boosts efficiency, yielding up to 80% token reduction and 75% cost savings for high-performing LLM agents while generally preserving or enhancing task success.
Skills learned demonstrate strong generalizability both across tasks of varying difficulty and between different LLM models, indicating the potential for robust, reusable procedural knowledge.

While beneficial, deeper hierarchical compositions (skills invoking other skills) proved less reliable and efficient than shallow skill libraries due to error propagation and increased debugging complexity.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks primarily focus on instance-level task success with static toolsets, overlooking agents' ability to abstract and reuse higher-level tool sequences.
Real-world agent workflows feature long-horizon tasks with recurring substructures, demanding compositional 'Skills' rather than repeated atomic tool calls for efficiency.
A lack of standardized methods to evaluate and encourage LLM agents' acquisition and generalization of complex, reusable tool compositions across diverse tasks.
results: Top-performing models like GPT-5.2 and Claude 4.5 Sonnet achieved average token reductions of 79% and 71% respectively in Skill Mode, with GPT-5.2's success rate improving from 87% to 90%.

Skills transferred across difficulty levels (e.g., from easy to hard tasks) within the same family exhibited high execution success rates (typically 97-100%), validating their reusable procedural structure.

High-quality skills created by capable models (e.g., Claude 4.5 Sonnet) showed 100% success when executed by other diverse models, highlighting that skill creator quality significantly impacts transferability and efficiency gains.
solution: The SkillCraft benchmark was developed with 126 tasks across six difficulty levels, specifically designed to contain recurring substructures that promote the discovery of compositional tool skills.
A "Skill Mode" evaluation protocol provides agents with four minimal primitives (`save skill`, `execute skill`, `list skills`, `get skill`) to manage, store, and invoke learned tool compositions.
A three-stage coding verifier ensures the syntactic correctness, runtime integrity, and quality of newly created skills before they are added to the agent's reusable skill library.
paper_summary.summary: SkillCraft introduces a benchmark and evaluation protocol designed to measure large language model agents' capacity for acquiring and reusing compositional tool skills. The framework demonstrates that stronger LLM agents can achieve up to 80% token reduction and significant cost savings while maintaining or improving task success rates by employing abstractable and reusable skills.

title: SkillCraft: Can LLM Agents Learn to Use Tools Skillfully?

abstract: LLM-based agents are increasingly capable of complex task execution, yet current agentic systems remain constrained by text-centric paradigms that struggle with long-horizon tasks due to fragile multi-turn dependencies and context drift. We present CaveAgent, a framework that shifts tool use from ``LLM-as-Text-Generator'' to ``LLM-as-Runtime-Operator.'' CaveAgent introduces a dual-stream architecture that inverts the conventional paradigm: rather than treating the LLM's text context as the primary workspace with tools as auxiliary, CaveAgent elevates the persistent Python runtime as the central locus of state, with a lightweight semantic stream serving as its orchestrator. Beyond leveraging code generation to resolve interdependent sub-tasks (e.g., loops, conditionals) in a single step, CaveAgent introduces \textit{Stateful Runtime Management}: it injects, manipulates, and retrieves complex Python objects (e.g., DataFrames, database connections) that persist across turns, unlike existing code-based approaches that remain text-bound. CaveAgent further provides a runtime-integrated skill management system that extends the Agent Skills open standard, enabling ecosystem interoperability through executable skill injections. This persistence mechanism serves as a high-fidelity external memory that reduces context drift in multi-turn interactions and preserves processed data for downstream applications without information loss. Evaluations show consistent improvement across challenging benchmarks, enabling CaveAgent to handle data scales that cause context overflow in both JSON-based and code-based agents. The accessible runtime state further provides programmatically verifiable feedback, enabling automated evaluation and reward signal generation without human annotation and establishing a structural foundation for future research in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR).

keyInsights: Delegating complex state management and intermediate data storage to a persistent, object-oriented runtime environment significantly reduces context drift, token overhead, and information loss for LLMs.
By adopting "Code as Action, State as Memory," LLMs can directly interact with and manipulate high-fidelity Python objects, enabling richer and more reliable operations than text-based serialization.

The dual-stream architecture and persistent runtime facilitate programmatic verifiability of agent states, laying a foundational architectural component for automated evaluation and future reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR).
originalProblem: Existing LLM tool use paradigms, including JSON-Schema function calling and prior code-based approaches, are hampered by a "textualization bottleneck" that necessitates serializing intermediate results into text.
